sanskrit_devanagari:
आश्चर्यवत्पश्यति कश्चिदेन-
माश्चर्यवद्वदति तथैव चान्यः ।
आश्चर्यवच्चैनमन्यः शृणोति
श्रुत्वाप्येनं वेद न चैव कश्चित् ॥ २९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
āścaryavat paśyati kaścid enam
āścaryavad vadati tathaiva cānyaḥ |
āścaryavac cainam anyaḥ śṛṇoti
śrutvāpy enaṁ veda na caiva kaścit || 29 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; line-breaks inserted between quarters in source pack — this verse is in trishtubh meter, four pādas)
exact (IAST text body identical to vedabase)

exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.029 = MBh_06,024.029). Body text identical. Note: this is a trishtubh-meter verse (11 syllables per pāda × 4 pādas = 44 total) — the only one in this block, marking the verse out structurally as well as thematically.

translations.verbatim_quote: Some look on the soul as amazing, some describe him as amazing, and some hear of him as amazing, while others, even after hearing about him, cannot understand him at all.
Some see the soul as amazing, some describe it as amazing, and some hear of the soul as amazing, while others, even on hearing, cannot understand it at all.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Since Gītopaniṣad is largely based on the principles of the Upaniṣads, it is not surprising to also find this passage in the Kaṭha Upaniṣad (1.2.7). The fact that the atomic soul is within the body of such a gigantic animal as the elephant, that it is also in the body of the microscopic germ.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.29 'This Self under discussion is inscrutable. Why should I blame you alone regarding a thing that is a source of delusion to all!' How is this Self inscrutable? Kascit, someone; pasyati, visualizes; enam, It, the Self; ascaryavat, as a wonder.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.29 as Krishna's honest acknowledgement that the Self is inscrutable — durvijñeya. The verse's structure (someone sees, someone speaks, someone hears, no one fully knows) marks the asymmetry between exposure and understanding. Krishna is not blaming Arjuna for not yet seeing; the Self is hard for everyone. Even hearing about it does not produce the knowing of it. The verse softens the polemic of the previous verses by acknowledging the limit of transmission.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.29 Among innumerable beings, someone, who by great austerity has got rid of sins and has increased his merits, realises this self possessing the above mentioned nature, which is wonderful and distinct in kind from all things other than Itself. Such a one speaks of It to another. Thus, someone hears of It.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as an honest description of how knowledge of the Self propagates through the world. A rare seer realises it; that seer transmits it; another hears it; and even after hearing, the knowing of it is not guaranteed. The verse is structurally a chain — seer, speaker, hearer, and (perhaps) one who comes to know. Each link is rare. The chain itself is rare. Hearing is not the same as knowing.

positions.rendering: even after hearing about him, cannot understand him at all
even on hearing, cannot understand it at all
the Self is durvijñeya — hard to know; even hearing does not produce knowing
even after hearing, the knowing is rare; the chain seer→speaker→hearer→knower has fragile final link
having heard, anyone (kaścit) does not indeed know

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All renderings agree on the surface meaning but the implication differs. Prabhupada and Mukundananda render 'kaścit' as a strong negative ('cannot understand at all'), implying near-universal failure. Shankara softens this — the Self is hard to know (durvijñeya), but the verse is descriptive rather than discouraging. Ramanuja's reading is the most carefully structured: the verse describes the rarity of each link in a four-step chain (seer, speaker, hearer, knower), with the final link being the rarest. The verse is not saying 'no one knows' — it is saying 'knowing it is not produced by hearing about it.' This distinction matters: Krishna is not telling Arjuna it is hopeless; he is telling Arjuna why even his own teaching has limits.

literal_meaning: Some see this [Self] as a wonder; another speaks of it as a wonder; another hears of it as a wonder; and even having heard, none truly knows it.
traditional_meaning_consensus: An interlude verse acknowledging the limit of knowledge transmission. Krishna pauses the polemical sequence (2.11-2.28, the imperishable doctrine) to note the asymmetry between exposure and understanding: seeing, speaking, hearing, knowing are four distinct events, and they do not chain reliably. The Self is durvijñeya — hard to know. The verse is structurally significant in two ways: (a) it is the only trishtubh-meter verse in this block (44 syllables vs. 32 of an anushtubh shloka), formally marking it out; (b) it softens the argument by acknowledging that even Krishna's teaching is limited by what can be transmitted. The commentarial tradition (Shankara, Ramanuja, Sridhara, Keshava) treats this as a key moment of intellectual humility within an otherwise polemical sequence. After this verse, Krishna concludes the imperishable section with 2.30 ('therefore do not grieve') and then pivots to the duty argument at 2.31.
